name: Jesse Marcel
description: Jesse Marcel Sr. (1907-1986) was a United States Air Force officer best known for his involvement in the Roswell incident of 1947. He was the intelligence officer for the 509th Bombardment Group based at Roswell Army Air Field in New Mexico. Marcel initially handled recovered debris from a ranch near Roswell, which he later claimed was extraterrestrial in origin. Proponents of the Roswell UFO incident theory cite Marcel's statements as primary evidence that the U.S. military recovered a crashed alien spacecraft. He described the debris as being unlike anything he had ever seen, including lightweight metallic materials with unusual properties, such as being incredibly strong and flexible. Marcel's claims gained prominence decades after the event, particularly through interviews and documentaries. Mainstream explanations attribute the recovered debris to Project Mogul, a top-secret U.S. Air Force project involving high-altitude balloons designed to detect Soviet nuclear tests. The official Air Force reports (1994 and 1997) assert that the debris was consistent with Mogul balloons and radar reflectors. Critics argue that Marcel's initial statements immediately after the incident aligned with the Air Force's initial press release about recovering a 'flying disc,' but his later, more detailed accounts about alien materials emerged much later, raising questions about the reliability and consistency of his narrative. Furthermore, inconsistencies in Marcel's descriptions of the debris over time are often highlighted by skeptics. Psychological factors, such as memory distortion and the influence of suggestion, are also considered as potential explanations for the evolution of Marcel's account.
subtitle: Roswell Incident's Key Witness

claims.claim: Marcel handled debris from a crashed alien spacecraft.
claims.evidence: Marcel's personal testimony and accounts of the debris' unusual properties (lightweight, strong, flexible).
claims.criticism: The official explanation attributes the debris to Project Mogul balloons. Inconsistencies in Marcel's accounts over time raise doubts about their reliability. Psychological factors may have influenced his memory.

claims.claim: The debris possessed characteristics unlike anything known at the time.
claims.evidence: Marcel's descriptions of the material's properties, including its resistance to damage and unusual markings.
claims.criticism: Skeptics argue that the described properties are not inconsistent with materials used in Project Mogul or other experimental technologies of the era. No independent verification of these properties exists.

claims.claim: The U.S. government engaged in a cover-up to conceal the true nature of the Roswell incident.
claims.evidence: Marcel's claim that the military initially acknowledged the recovery of a 'flying disc' but later changed the story to a weather balloon. Subsequent witness testimonies support claims of a cover-up.
claims.criticism: The Air Force's reports detail the evolution of the official explanation and address claims of a cover-up, citing the need to protect classified information about Project Mogul. Skeptics argue that there is no credible evidence of a deliberate conspiracy.

keyFigures.name: Jesse Marcel Sr.
keyFigures.role: Intelligence Officer who handled the debris and later claimed it was extraterrestrial.

keyFigures.name: James McAndrew
keyFigures.role: U.S. Air Force officer who authored official reports on the Roswell incident, attributing the debris to Project Mogul.

timeline.event: Roswell incident occurs; Marcel handles debris.
Stanton Friedman interviews Marcel, sparking renewed interest in the Roswell incident.
Marcel's accounts gain prominence in UFO literature and documentaries.
U.S. Air Force releases reports attributing the Roswell debris to Project Mogul.

sources.author: Kevin D. Randle and Donald R. Schmitt
sources.title: Kevin D. Randle and Donald R. Schmitt

sources.author: Karl T. Pflock
sources.work: Roswell: Inconvenient Facts and the Will to Believe
sources.title: Karl T. Pflock
